topic: Global Development & Connections (Health, Human Rights / Migration, Identity)
subject: geography

items.q: Is development the same as economic growth?
items.correct: No — growth means rising GDP, while development also includes health, education, rights and quality of life; an economy can grow while wellbeing stagnates.
items.misconception: A country with rising GDP is automatically becoming more developed.
items.random_wrong: Development means a country is physically growing in land area.
items.why: Conflating growth and development loses marks; the HDI exists precisely because income alone misleads.

items.correct: No — the majority of international migrants move for work, study or family reasons; refugees fleeing persecution are a minority of total migrant flows.
items.misconception: Almost all migration is forced displacement caused by war.
items.random_wrong: Migrants are people who travel abroad on holiday.
items.why: Media coverage of crises leads students to overestimate forced migration's share of total movement.

items.q: Does signing a human-rights treaty guarantee rights are protected?
items.correct: No — ratification creates legal commitments, but enforcement depends on national governments, courts and political will, so practice often lags far behind paper commitments.
items.misconception: Once a country signs a treaty, abuses stop.
items.random_wrong: Human-rights treaties only apply to island nations.
items.why: Students assume international law is self-enforcing; the gap between commitment and enforcement is a core theme of this topic.

items.correct: Income per capita, life expectancy and education
items.misconception: Birth rate, death rate and migration rate
items.random_wrong: GDP, exports and the exchange rate
items.why: Correct answer: Income per capita, life expectancy and education. “Birth rate, death rate and migration rate” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A refugee is best defined as someone who:
items.correct: Flees across a border owing to a well-founded fear of persecution or conflict
items.misconception: Moves abroad mainly to earn higher wages
items.random_wrong: Moves to another region within their own country
items.why: Correct answer: Flees across a border owing to a well-founded fear of persecution or conflict. “Moves abroad mainly to earn higher wages” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in:
items.why: It's 1948. “1919” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is an example of 'hard power' intervention?
items.misconception: Cultural influence through films and media
items.why: It's Military action. “Cultural influence through films and media” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Universal Declaration of Human Rights adopted by the UN General Assembly?
items.why: The real figure is 1948 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
